exemplar.title: How has social media affected the way people communicate and express themselves in spoken English across North America in the 21st century, and which psychological theories help to explain this impact?

detailedExemplarData.title: How has social media affected the way people communicate and express themselves in spoken English across North America in the 21st century, and which psychological theories help to explain this impact?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Topic is communicated clearly and consistently, with effective use of examples to anchor the discussion.
Research question is explicitly stated and regularly referenced, helping to guide the exploration.
weaknesses: Methodological explanation is minimal, with no justification of source selection or primary data collection strategy.
Scope remains overly broad (geographical, temporal, registers, age groups) and is not bounded, causing occasional drift.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the essay communicates its topic and research question accurately, the methodology is under-developed and lacks evidence of informed selection. This places the work in the lower half of the 3â4 band, meriting a 3.

strengths: Core psychological theories are summarised accurately and landmark studies are cited.
Subject-specific terminology is used correctly most of the time, lending disciplinary coherence.
weaknesses: Several key terms lack primary-source citations, and some definitions (e.g., anapodoton, SMET) are mis-applied.
Dependence on nonâpeer-reviewed sources for linguistic concepts weakens academic rigor.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates good understanding and generally relevant source application, but inconsistent precision and secondary citations prevent attainment of the top band. A score of 3 reflects this level.

strengths: Research draws on a moderate range of sources that are mostly relevant to the question.
Analysis follows a clear mechanism-to-outcome logic and is supported by examples, yielding coherent conclusions.
weaknesses: Evaluation is largely descriptive with limited critical appraisal of alternative explanations or methodological limitations.
Discussion does not sufficiently synthesise counter-evidence and misses deeper critical engagement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical thinking shows good relevance of research and clear analytical structure but lacks depth in evaluation. It sits at the lower end of the 4â6 band, justifying a 4.

strengths: Basic structure (title, headings, pagination) is in place, aiding navigation.
weaknesses: Table of contents is incomplete and citation formatting is inconsistent.
Use of in-text hyperlinks and uneven reference styling detracts from professional presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets minimal requirements but contains significant formatting lapses. While it falls within the 1â2 band, the number and severity of issues justify the lower score of 1.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key terms such as âsocial identityâ, âvicarious reinforcementâ and âcultivationâ are used correctly, giving the essay disciplinary coherence.  Nonetheless, mis-definitions (e.g., "anapodoton") and occasional acronym errors (SMET) show inconsistent precision.  The largely accurate but not flawless usage aligns with the 3-4 band, meriting a score 3.

Essential layout elements (title, word count, headings, pagination) are present, facilitating navigation.  Nevertheless, citation style is inconsistent, hyperlinks appear in-text, and the table of contents is incomplete.  Such lapses mean formatting is only partially correct, fitting the 1-2 band (score 2).

The student summarises core psychological theories accurately and cites landmark studies (Tajfel, Bandura, Gerbner).  However, linguistic scholarship is thin, and several citations are secondary or mis-applied.  Evidence is mainly illustrative rather than critically integrated, so knowledge is good but not consistently excellent, fitting the lower end of the 3-4 band (score 3).

Methodological explanation is limited to a list-style literature review; there is no justification of source selection, no primary data collection, and no discussion of how evidence will be weighed.  Sources appear to be gathered opportunistically (e.g., YouTube videos, study guides) rather than through a stated strategy.  This demonstrates only minimal evidence of informed selection and sits firmly in the 1-2 band (score 2).

The student states the topic explicitly in the title, introduction and section headings, and maintains a clear psychological frame (SIT, SCT, cultivation) throughout, allowing the reader to understand the purpose with little effort.  Examples of language change (e.g., âttylâ, âbrain-rotâ) are interwoven to keep the discussion anchored in the chosen phenomenon.  However, the geographic (all of North America) and temporal (entire 21st century) scope remains very broad and no boundaries are set for spoken registers or age groups, so focus occasionally drifts.  Overall, communication is accurate and effective, meeting the 3-4 band, and the stronger clarity places it at the top of that band (score 4).

A moderate range of journal articles, textbooks and media sources is cited, and most references link to the research question.  Yet several are popular websites or duplicated, and some key linguistic studies are missing.  This reflects adequate, mostly relevant research rather than comprehensive, placing the work at the bottom of the 4-6 band (score 4).

The research question is clearly stated and consistently referenced, showing a deliberate attempt to steer the essay.  Nevertheless, it bundles two distinct aims (descriptive effects + theoretical explanation) and covers a vast population, so the discussion becomes diffuse in places.  Because the wording is clear but only partially focused and tightly connected to the analysis, the work fits the middle of the 3-4 band (score 3).

The essay follows the conventional EE structure with title page, contents, introduction, main body, conclusion and references, ensuring basic coherence.  Minor issuesâsuch as a contents page lacking full page numbers and occasional section imbalanceâindicate that structural conventions are only partly met.  This corresponds to a satisfactory but not fully appropriate structure (band 1-2, score 2).

The discussion reiterates main points and recognises some limitations (e.g., language used mainly in informal contexts), but evaluation of alternative explanations, methodological flaws or real-world implications is brief.  Argumentation is therefore limited and largely uncritical, placing it at the top of the 1-3 band (score 3).

The argument follows a clear mechanismâoutcome logic and regularly links theory to examples of language change; however, sections are largely descriptive and seldom weigh counter-evidence.  Conclusions are sometimes asserted without fresh synthesis of data.  This represents clear but only moderately supported analysis, matching the lower end of the 4-6 band (score 4).

annotations.comment: The table of contents includes main section titles but omits subheadings and complete page references, hindering easy navigation. Expand entries to reflect all sections and sub-sections.

The definition of social media is comprehensive but does not specify which registers, age groups, or contexts will be analysed, weakening the essayâs methodological focus. Define these boundaries explicitly.

Abramsâ conformity study is well explained, but its connection to real-time language adoption on social media remains superficial. Provide contemporary examples or small-scale data to illustrate the mechanism.
The four SCT conditions are listed but lack a direct citation to Banduraâs original study. Include the primary source reference and brief methodology summary to strengthen academic rigor.

The description of Tajfelâs experiment is detailed but lacks critique of how an artificial minimal group setup maps onto complex, dynamic social media communities. Discuss limitations and transferability.
Discussion of âlinguistic conformityâ relies on an online glossary rather than peer-reviewed research. Seek out and cite a scholarly article on linguistic adaptation for stronger source validity.
The connection between Banduraâs BoBo doll findings and language imitation on social media is asserted without direct evidence. Incorporate specific language sample analyses or quantitative data.
The acronym SMET is introduced incorrectly and without proper definition. Verify the original term, define it accurately, and cite its academic source.
The term âcognitive misersâ is used without citation. Provide the original reference (e.g., Fiske & Taylor) when introducing established psychological concepts.
The conclusion accurately summarises arguments but offers little in the way of implications, limitations, or suggestions for future research. Include these to enhance evaluative strength.
The repeated use of âcognitive miserlinessâ varies from earlier phrasing; ensure consistent terminology or define variations when first used.
Citing TikTok trends is relevant but unsubstantiated by usage statistics or trend analyses. Consider including platform analytics or survey results to support trend impact claims.
The discussion omits counterexamples where social media slang fails to integrate into spoken language. Address conditions that inhibit change to demonstrate balanced evaluation.
The Black Lives Matter example is engaging but presented without contrasting cases, limiting evaluative depth. Compare with another movement to illustrate variability in linguistic adoption.

The research question covers two distinct aims and an extensive geographic and temporal scope without clear boundaries, making the focus diffuse. Consider narrowing the population, timeframe, or registers to sharpen focus and strengthen coherence.
The word count is provided at the start and remains under the maximum 4,000 words, satisfying the formatting requirement.
The title and topic are clearly stated at the beginning of the report, meeting the formatting criterion for presentation of the investigationâs title.
Page numbering in the contents does not align with the listed subdivisions (e.g., missing page numbers for theory subsections), reducing clarity. Ensure all section levels are included correctly.

The minimal group paradigm is described thoroughly but its direct relevance to language change in social media contexts is asserted rather than critically analysed. Provide concrete linguistic examples to bridge theory and data.
Terms like âhomogeneity biasâ and âpositive distinctionâ are introduced without illustrative examples from language use. Add specific spoken or textual examples to demonstrate these concepts.
The term âanapodotonâ appears without linguistic citation or clear definition. Include a precise definition and reference from a reputable linguistic source to ensure accurate terminology.

The link between in-group bias and the integration of online linguistic markers into speech is implied but unsupported by empirical data. Incorporate corpus or survey evidence to substantiate this claim.
The role of âinfluencerâ communities is central yet undefined; clarify how these groups are identified and selected, and justify this operationalization within your methodology.
Cultivation theoryâs longitudinal methodology is only described generally. Add detail on Gerbnerâs sampling and analysis methods to clarify how media exposure effects were measured.
The link between shifts in media-cultivated attitudes and speech patterns is asserted without theoretical depth. Expand on how perceptual changes translate into conversational language practices.
The list styling in the final reference section is inconsistent (ordered list without numbers). Apply proper numbering or bulleting to enhance readability.
The âbrain rotâ example is contemporary but lacks evidence of oral use. Include survey or corpus data demonstrating its spoken prevalence.
The citation for David Crystalâs quote appears inconsistently formatted and missing details. Align with your chosen citation style for uniformity.
Duplicate entries for the Pamoja Student Guide appear, indicating reference management issues. Remove duplicates and consolidate citations accurately.
The works cited list mixes citation formats (e.g., journal, websites, YouTube), disrupting consistency. Standardise entries according to a single style guide.
Several online sources lack access dates or full publication details. Verify each reference includes complete information to meet academic standards.
